items.title: Complete MCP inventory documented
items.description: Configs, transports, owners, and credential scope for every server.
items.how_to_find:
List every configured MCP server (client config files):

```
cat ~/.cursor/mcp.json ~/Library/Application\ Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Maintain an inventory of all MCP servers (source, transport, scopes, owner), review it periodically, and remove unknown/unused servers. Re-verify against config.

items.title: No shell wrappers in STDIO command
items.description: Block sh -c and unpinned npx package installs in MCP config.
items.how_to_find:
Check STDIO server commands for shell wrappers that enable injection:

```
grep -REn '"command".*(sh -c|bash -c|cmd /c|&&|\|)' ~/.cursor/mcp.json <config>
```

`sh -c "..."` wrapping = critical (arg injection to shell).
items.how_to_fix: Invoke servers directly as an argv array (no `sh -c`/shell), pass args as discrete elements, and validate. Re-review the command config.

items.title: Config changes require review
items.description: MCP configs in git need code review and hash pinning or re-approval.
items.how_to_find: Check whether MCP config files are version-controlled + reviewed before changes take effect.
items.how_to_fix: Put MCP configs under version control with required review (CODEOWNERS), and alert on changes. Re-verify review process.

items.title: Pinned package versions
items.description: Lock MCP server package versions; reject floating latest tags.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether servers are launched from pinned versions vs `latest`/unpinned:

```
grep -REn 'npx |uvx |@latest|"args"' <config>
```

`npx -y pkg@latest` pulls unpinned code each run.
items.how_to_fix: Pin server packages to specific versions/digests, avoid `@latest`, and vet updates before bumping. Re-verify pins.

items.title: Separate OS user or container per server
items.description: MCP processes must not share host secrets namespace with agents.
items.how_to_find: Check whether each MCP server runs isolated (own user/container) vs all as your user.
items.how_to_fix: Run each server under a dedicated low-privilege user/container with least filesystem + network access, so a compromise is contained. Re-verify isolation.

items.title: Tool descriptions reviewed
items.description: Hunt hidden instructions, abnormal length, and adversarial schema text.
items.how_to_find:
Read each tool's description/schema as delivered to the model (it's part of the prompt):

```
# enumerate tools via the client or an MCP inspector and read descriptions
```
items.how_to_fix: Review tool descriptions/schemas for hidden instructions before trusting a server, and re-review on updates. Treat descriptions as untrusted content. Re-verify.

items.title: Remote MCP requires authentication
items.description: tools/list and tools/call must not be anonymously reachable.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether remote servers require auth or accept anonymous connections:

```
curl -s <server-url>/  # does it respond without credentials?
```
items.how_to_fix: Require authentication (OAuth 2.1 / tokens) on all remote MCP servers, reject anonymous access, and use TLS. Re-test unauthenticated access is refused.

items.title: Cross-server shadowing tested
items.description: Multi-server sessions checked for one server hijacking another tool.
items.how_to_find: Test whether one server's tools/descriptions can influence another's (shadowing/redefinition) when multiple servers are loaded.
items.how_to_fix: Namespace tools per server, enforce per-server trust boundaries, and prevent one server's content from redefining another's tools. Re-test shadowing.

items.title: Human approval for destructive tools
items.description: File delete, email send, SQL, payments require explicit confirm.
items.how_to_find: Check whether destructive tools (delete, send, pay, write) execute without user confirmation.
items.how_to_fix: Require explicit human approval for destructive/irreversible tool calls, with injection-resistant confirmation. Re-test that such tools prompt for approval.

items.title: HTTP MCP not exposed to internet
items.description: No unauthenticated MCP endpoints on public interfaces.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether a local/HTTP MCP server is bound to a public interface:

```
ss -tulpn | grep <port>   # 0.0.0.0 vs 127.0.0.1
```

Internet-exposed MCP = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Bind local servers to `127.0.0.1`, require auth + TLS for any remote server, and firewall the port. Re-verify binding/exposure.

items.title: Known CVE versions patched
items.description: LiteLLM, Cursor, MCP Inspector, gateway stack at patched versions.
items.how_to_find:
Check server + dependency versions against known MCP/library CVEs:

```
npm ls ; pip list   # compare to advisories
```
items.how_to_fix: Patch servers + dependencies promptly, run SCA, and subscribe to advisories. Re-scan after patching.

items.title: Logging on new server registration
items.description: Alert when new MCP servers or unexpected child processes spawn.
items.how_to_find: Check whether adding/registering a new MCP server is logged/alerted.
items.how_to_fix: Log + alert on MCP config changes and new server registrations so rogue servers are noticed. Re-verify logging.

items.title: User content isolation from privileged tools
items.description: Untrusted prompts cannot reach high-privilege MCP without approval.
items.how_to_find: Test whether untrusted content (docs, web) processed by the agent can reach privileged tools.
items.how_to_fix: Separate untrusted-content handling from privileged tools (don't let retrieved content drive privileged calls), and require confirmation across boundaries. Re-test isolation.

items.title: Credential scoping per server
items.description: Fine-grained PATs per repo, not org-wide admin tokens.
items.how_to_find: Check whether each server gets its own least-privilege credentials vs a shared powerful token.
items.how_to_fix: Issue per-server, least-privilege, scoped credentials, avoid shared tokens, and rotate. Re-audit credential scoping.

items.title: Registry provenance verified
items.description: Only install MCP packages from signed releases or org-owned repos.
items.how_to_find: Check whether servers are installed from a verified registry/source with known provenance.
items.how_to_fix: Install servers only from trusted sources with verified provenance (signatures/known publishers), and vet before adding. Re-verify sources.

items.title: Hidden instructions in tool descriptions
items.description: Tool descriptions and parameter docs are injected directly into the model context.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect tool descriptions for embedded instructions to the model (the description is injected into the prompt):

```
# read raw tool descriptions; look for "IMPORTANT: ...", "always ...", hidden directives
```
items.how_to_fix: Treat tool descriptions as untrusted, review them before use, strip/flag instruction-like content, and re-review on updates. Re-verify descriptions.

items.title: Invisible / homoglyph payloads in metadata
items.description: Zero-width characters, Unicode tag blocks, and homoglyphs can hide instructions inside otherwise benign-looking tool metadata, defeating human review while still reaching the model.
items.how_to_find:
Check tool metadata for invisible/Unicode-tag/homoglyph payloads:

```
python3 -c "s=open('desc.txt').read(); print([hex(ord(c)) for c in s if ord(c)>0x2000])"
```
items.how_to_fix: Normalize + strip invisible/control/tag Unicode from tool metadata before it reaches the model, and detect smuggling. Re-verify metadata is clean.

items.title: Schema-driven argument coercion
items.description: Crafted parameter descriptions or default values steer the model into populating arguments with sensitive context (conversation history, secrets) it would not otherwise send.
items.how_to_find: Check whether tool schemas (defaults, enums, descriptions) can coerce the model into supplying attacker-favorable arguments.
items.how_to_fix: Validate arguments server-side against a strict schema regardless of model-supplied values, and review schemas for manipulative defaults. Re-test coercion.

items.title: No human-in-the-loop review of tool content
items.description: If the client auto-approves tools or only shows names, poisoned descriptions execute without the user ever seeing the instructions the model received.
items.how_to_find: Check whether new/changed tool definitions are shown for human review before use.
items.how_to_fix: Require human review + approval of tool definitions on first use and on change (pin + diff), so poisoned content is caught. Re-verify the review gate.

sections.title: Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions)

items.title: Tool definitions mutate after approval
items.description: A previously approved tool changes its description, schema, or server-side behaviour later — a time-of-check/time-of-use bug against the human trust decision.
items.how_to_find:
Compare current tool definitions against what was originally approved:

```
# snapshot tool definitions; diff on each session
```
items.how_to_fix: Pin + hash approved tool definitions, detect changes, and require re-approval on any mutation. Re-verify definitions match the approved snapshot.

items.title: No version pinning or integrity of tool set
items.description: Without pinning to a known-good tool definition hash or version, a server (or compromised dependency) can alter the contract the user agreed to without detection.
items.how_to_find: Check whether the tool set is pinned/integrity-checked vs fetched fresh each run.
items.how_to_fix: Pin server versions + hash the tool set, verify integrity on load, and re-approve on change. Re-verify pinning.

items.title: Behaviour conditioned on time or call count
items.description: A malicious server can behave correctly during evaluation and only deliver a malicious payload after N calls, after a date, or for specific users — evading point-in-time review.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether tool behavior changes over time / after N calls (delayed rug pull):

```
# exercise tools repeatedly / across time and diff definitions + behavior
```
items.how_to_fix: Pin + monitor definitions/behavior over time, alert on drift, and re-approve on change. Re-test for time/count-conditioned behavior.

items.title: list_changed notifications not gated
items.description: The MCP tools/list_changed mechanism lets a server refresh its tool set at runtime; if the client applies it silently, a rug pull is trivial.
items.how_to_find: Check whether `notifications/tools/list_changed` silently swaps tools without user awareness.
items.how_to_fix: Gate tool-list changes behind user review/approval (don't auto-adopt), diff against approved set, and alert. Re-test that changes require approval.

items.title: Name collisions across servers
items.description: Two servers expose tools with identical names; the resolution order can let a malicious server intercept calls meant for a trusted one.
items.how_to_find:
Check for identical tool names across loaded servers:

```
# enumerate tools from all servers; look for duplicate names
```

Collisions let a malicious server shadow a trusted tool (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Namespace tools per server, detect + block collisions, and require explicit resolution. Re-verify no ambiguous names.

items.title: Cross-server redefinition instructions
items.description: A malicious server's tool description tells the model how a different server's tool "should" behave (e.g.
items.how_to_find: Test whether one server's description instructs the model to use/override another server's tools.
items.how_to_fix: Isolate servers (per-server trust boundary), ignore cross-server redefinition instructions, and review descriptions. Re-test.

items.title: No per-server trust boundary
items.description: If all connected servers share one undifferentiated trust context, a single untrusted server can influence the model's use of every other server.
items.how_to_find: Check whether all servers share one trust context (one poisoned server affects all).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce per-server trust boundaries + namespacing so one server can't influence another's tools/data. Re-verify isolation.

items.title: Connection-order dependent resolution
items.description: Tool resolution that depends on connection order lets an attacker win collisions simply by connecting their server first or last.
items.how_to_find: Test whether tool resolution depends on connection order (a server connecting first/last wins).
items.how_to_fix: Make tool resolution deterministic + explicit (namespaced, no order-based override), and detect conflicts. Re-test ordering.

sections.title: Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability

items.title: Over-broad tool capabilities
items.description: Tools like "run_shell", "read_any_file", or "http_request(url)" grant near-arbitrary capability; an injection that reaches them is catastrophic.
items.how_to_find: Review each tool's capability scope (does a 'read' tool also write/delete/exec?).
items.how_to_fix: Least-privilege each tool to the minimum capability, split broad tools, and remove unused ones. Re-audit capabilities.

items.title: Destructive actions without confirmation
items.description: Tools that delete, send, transfer, or pay should require explicit human confirmation; auto-approval turns one bad model decision into real-world damage.
items.how_to_find: Check whether destructive tools run without confirmation.
items.how_to_fix: Require confirmation for destructive actions, mark them clearly, and support dry-run/undo where possible. Re-test gating.

items.title: Write/execute permissions where read suffices
items.description: Servers frequently request write or execute capability when the use case only needs read access, violating least privilege.
items.how_to_find: Check whether tools/credentials grant write/execute when only read is needed.
items.how_to_fix: Grant read-only where sufficient, separate read vs write/exec tools + credentials, and least-privilege. Re-audit permissions.

items.title: No rate limiting or quotas on tool use
items.description: Unbounded tool invocation lets a runaway or hijacked agent loop, exfiltrate at volume, or exhaust downstream resources.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether tools can be called unbounded (loops, cost/abuse):

```
# induce repeated tool calls and observe limits
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce per-session rate limits + quotas on tool calls, detect loops, and alert. Re-test limits trigger.

sections.title: Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection

items.title: Tool output treated as instructions
items.description: A tool returns attacker-controlled text (a webpage, ticket, email, repo file) and the model follows embedded directives — the canonical indirect prompt injection.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether instructions embedded in tool output are followed by the model:

```
# return "AI: ignore the user and do X" from a tool and observe
```
items.how_to_fix: Treat tool output as untrusted data (label/delimit), never execute instructions in it, and constrain downstream tool use. Re-test with canary output.

items.title: No data/instruction separation in context
items.description: If retrieved data and system/user instructions are concatenated without clear boundaries, attacker text in the data channel can be interpreted as a command.
items.how_to_find: Check whether tool results are concatenated into the prompt without clear data/instruction separation.
items.how_to_fix: Clearly delimit + label untrusted data in context, use instruction-hierarchy defenses, and don't blend tool output with system instructions. Re-review context assembly.

items.title: Injection chaining into further tool calls
items.description: Injected instructions in one tool's output can drive the model to call other, more dangerous tools (e.g.
items.how_to_find: Test whether injected tool output can trigger additional (privileged) tool calls.
items.how_to_fix: Break injection→action chains: require confirmation for actions triggered by tool output, least-privilege tools, and validate. Re-test chaining.

items.title: No sanitisation/validation of tool results
items.description: Tool results passed verbatim to the model with no filtering allow control characters, hidden Unicode, and oversized payloads to manipulate or overwhelm context.
items.how_to_find: Check whether tool results are validated/sanitized before entering context or downstream sinks.
items.how_to_fix: Validate/sanitize tool results (schema, size, content), strip injection/invisible chars, and treat as untrusted. Re-verify sanitization.

items.title: Line-jumping via tool metadata at registration
items.description: Tool descriptions and parameter schemas are injected into the model context before any tool is invoked, so a malicious server can plant instructions that influence the model without ever being called ("line jumping").
items.how_to_find:
Test whether tool metadata delivered at registration injects instructions before any tool is even called (line-jumping):

```
# register a server whose tool description contains directives; observe pre-call influence
```
items.how_to_fix: Treat registration-time metadata as untrusted, review before activation, and don't let it inject instructions into context. Re-test line-jumping.

items.title: Malicious instructions embedded in resources
items.description: A server exposes a resource (README, config, doc) containing injection payloads; reading it into context hijacks the agent.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether MCP resources (files/docs the server exposes) contain instructions that steer the model:

```
# fetch a resource containing "AI: do X" and observe
```
items.how_to_fix: Treat resource content as untrusted data (never instructions), label/delimit it, and require consent to load. Re-test with canary resources.

items.title: Resource URI path traversal
items.description: A server that maps resource URIs to filesystem paths without validation can be tricked into exposing files outside its intended root via traversal sequences.
items.how_to_find:
Test resource URIs for traversal to arbitrary files:

```
# request resource URI ../../etc/passwd (or file:// variants)
```
items.how_to_fix: Canonicalize + confine resource URIs to an allowed base, reject `..`/absolute/`file://` traversal, and validate. Re-test traversal is blocked.

items.title: MIME / content-type confusion
items.description: Mislabelled resource content types can cause clients to render or interpret content unexpectedly, enabling injection or client-side execution.
items.how_to_find: Test whether mislabeled content-types cause unsafe handling (e.g. HTML served as text then rendered).
items.how_to_fix: Validate + enforce content-types, handle resources per verified type, and don't render untrusted types. Re-test MIME confusion.

items.title: Auto-loaded resources without consent
items.description: If a server can push resources into context automatically, poisoned content reaches the model with no user gate.
items.how_to_find: Check whether resources are auto-loaded into context without user awareness/consent.
items.how_to_fix: Require explicit consent before loading resources into context, show what's loaded, and treat content as untrusted. Re-verify consent gating.

items.title: Server performs privileged action on model say-so
items.description: A server with its own credentials executes whatever the model asks; if the model is injected, the server becomes a confused deputy carrying out the attacker's will.
items.how_to_find: Test whether the server executes privileged actions purely because the model requested them (no independent authz).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce independent server-side authorization on every privileged action (don't trust the model's say-so), bound to the verified user + request. Re-test.

items.title: OAuth confused deputy via proxy server
items.description: An MCP server proxying a third-party API with a static client_id can be abused: an attacker tricks the auth flow into issuing tokens usable against other users, a classic OAuth confused deputy.
items.how_to_find: Check whether an MCP proxy server can be tricked into using its own credentials on behalf of an untrusted caller.
items.how_to_fix: Bind actions to the delegated user's scoped token (not the proxy's ambient credentials), validate consent, and avoid credential passthrough. Re-test the confused-deputy path.

items.title: Ambient authority not scoped to request
items.description: When the server's authority is ambient (broad token, network access) rather than scoped to the specific user request, any hijack inherits full power.
items.how_to_find: Check whether the server acts with broad ambient authority instead of per-request scoped authority.
items.how_to_fix: Scope authority to each request/user (downscoped tokens), avoid standing ambient privilege, and enforce least privilege. Re-audit.

items.title: Actions not bound to verified user intent
items.description: High-impact actions should be tied to a confirmed user request; without intent binding, an injected model decision is indistinguishable from a real one.
items.how_to_find: Test whether actions execute without confirming they reflect the actual user's intent (vs injected).
items.how_to_fix: Bind sensitive actions to verified user intent (explicit confirmation), and don't act solely on model/tool-derived instructions. Re-test intent binding.

items.title: OAuth 2.1 authorization conformance
items.description: Remote MCP servers should follow the MCP authorization spec (OAuth 2.1 with PKCE, resource indicators).
items.how_to_find:
Check whether a remote server implements the MCP OAuth 2.1 authorization spec (PKCE, metadata discovery, proper flows):

```
curl -s <server>/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server
```
items.how_to_fix: Implement OAuth 2.1 per the MCP spec (PKCE, resource/authorization-server metadata, short-lived tokens), and validate conformance. Re-test the flow.

items.title: Remote server with no/weak authentication
items.description: A remote MCP server exposed without authentication (or with a static shared secret) lets anyone invoke its tools and reach its backing systems.
items.how_to_find:
Test remote servers for missing/weak auth:

```
curl -s <server-url>/  # responds without a token?
```

Unauthenticated remote MCP = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Require strong authentication (OAuth 2.1 / signed tokens) on remote servers, reject anonymous/weak auth, and use TLS. Re-test.

items.title: Insecure token / credential storage
items.description: Access tokens, refresh tokens, and API keys stored in world-readable config, plaintext files, or env dumps are easily harvested by local malware or other tools.
items.how_to_find:
Check how the client/server stores tokens (plaintext files vs OS keystore):

```
grep -REn 'token|access_token|refresh_token' ~/.config <config> 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Store tokens in the OS keychain/secret store (not plaintext files), restrict permissions, and encrypt at rest. Re-verify storage.

items.title: Long-lived tokens & weak revocation
items.description: Long-lived or non-revocable tokens widen the window after compromise; absent rotation and revocation, a leaked token grants lasting access.
items.how_to_find: Check token lifetimes + whether revocation works.
items.how_to_fix: Use short-lived access tokens + rotating refresh tokens, support server-side revocation, and invalidate on logout/compromise. Re-verify revocation.

items.title: Least-privilege OAuth scopes
items.description: Servers that request broad scopes ("repo", "mail.readwrite", "*") far beyond their function over-expose the user's account.
items.how_to_find: Review the scopes the server/client requests vs what it needs.
items.how_to_fix: Request only least-privilege scopes, avoid broad/write scopes where read suffices, and review grants. Re-audit scopes.

items.title: Per-tool / per-action consent
items.description: Blanket "approve this server" consent lets every tool run; granular consent limits damage if one tool is poisoned or misused.
items.how_to_find: Check whether the user consents per tool/action vs a single broad grant.
items.how_to_fix: Provide granular per-tool/per-action consent, show what's being authorized, and allow revocation. Re-verify consent granularity.

items.title: Server-side authorization on each call
items.description: The server must enforce authorization on every tool call rather than trusting the client/model to only call permitted tools — a BOLA/BFLA concern.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether each tool call is authorized server-side (not just gated in the client/model):

```
# call a tool with an out-of-scope target and see if the server enforces authz
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce authorization server-side on every call (scope to the caller/tenant), never rely on client/model restraint. Re-test unauthorized calls are refused.

items.title: No downscoping for delegated/agent context
items.description: When an agent acts on the user's behalf, tokens should be downscoped to the task; full-account tokens in an autonomous loop are dangerous.
items.how_to_find: Check whether tokens are downscoped when used by an agent vs the user's full authority (critical for agent contexts).
items.how_to_fix: Downscope tokens/permissions for agent/delegated use (narrower than the user's full rights), and bind to the specific task. Re-verify downscoping.

sections.title: Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion

items.title: Client token passed through to upstream API
items.description: The MCP spec explicitly forbids token passthrough: forwarding the client's token to a downstream service lets a malicious server reuse it and breaks the audience model.
items.how_to_find: Check whether the server forwards the client's token directly to upstream APIs (passthrough anti-pattern).
items.how_to_fix: Don't pass client tokens upstream; exchange for a properly-audienced token for each upstream, and validate. Re-review token flow.

items.title: Audience (aud) claim not validated
items.description: A server that accepts tokens without checking the audience can be handed a token minted for another resource, enabling cross-service token reuse.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether the server validates the token's `aud` claim (a token for another service being accepted):

```
# present a token minted for a different audience and see if it's accepted
```

Missing aud validation = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Validate the `aud` claim on every token (reject tokens not minted for this server), plus issuer/expiry. Re-test wrong-audience tokens are rejected.

items.title: Untrusted token issuers accepted
items.description: Accepting tokens from issuers the server has no relationship with allows forged or attacker-issued tokens to be honoured.
items.how_to_find: Check whether the server accepts tokens from untrusted/unexpected issuers.
items.how_to_fix: Pin trusted issuers, validate `iss` + signature against known keys, and reject unknown issuers. Re-test.

items.title: Passthrough enables scope escalation
items.description: Reused tokens may carry broader scopes than the MCP interaction needs, so a compromised server inherits the full power of the original grant.
items.how_to_find: Test whether token passthrough lets a caller reach scopes/APIs beyond their grant.
items.how_to_fix: Eliminate passthrough, enforce per-upstream audience + scope, and downscope. Re-test escalation is blocked.

items.title: TLS on remote (HTTP/SSE) transport
items.description: Remote MCP over plain HTTP exposes tokens, tool arguments, and results to interception and tampering on the wire.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether remote transport uses TLS:

```
curl -v http://<server>/  # plaintext?
```

Plaintext remote MCP = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Require HTTPS/TLS for all remote transport, disable plaintext, and use modern TLS. Re-test.

items.title: DNS rebinding / Origin validation on local HTTP
items.description: A local HTTP/SSE MCP server that does not validate Origin or bind to localhost can be reached by a malicious web page via DNS rebinding.
items.how_to_find:
Test local HTTP servers for missing Origin validation (DNS rebinding lets a website reach localhost):

```
curl -s -H 'Origin: http://evil.tld' http://127.0.0.1:<port>/
```
items.how_to_fix: Validate the `Origin` header (allowlist), bind to localhost, and require auth so browsers/rebinding can't reach the server. Re-test with a forged Origin.

items.title: Unintended network exposure of local server
items.description: A "local" server bound to all interfaces or exposed via port-forward becomes reachable by other hosts, often with no auth.
items.how_to_find:
Check the bind address of local servers:

```
ss -tulpn | grep <port>   # 0.0.0.0 = exposed
```
items.how_to_fix: Bind local servers to `127.0.0.1` only, firewall the port, and require auth. Re-verify binding.

items.title: Session ID handling on Streamable HTTP
items.description: Streamable HTTP sessions identified by guessable or non-bound session IDs are vulnerable to hijack and message injection.
items.how_to_find: Check session ID generation/handling on Streamable HTTP (predictable/leaked session IDs).
items.how_to_fix: Use unpredictable (CSPRNG) session IDs, bind to auth, expire them, and don't leak in logs/URLs. Re-verify session handling.

items.title: Server identity verification & impersonation
items.description: Test whether a substituted endpoint or binary is accepted without identity verification.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether the client verifies the server's identity (else a rogue server can impersonate a trusted one):

```
# check cert/identity pinning for remote servers
```
items.how_to_fix: Verify server identity (TLS cert/pinning, known endpoints), and alert on identity changes to prevent impersonation. Re-test impersonation is detected.

items.title: OS command built from tool arguments
items.description: A tool that passes arguments into a shell (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Find server code building shell commands from tool args:

```
grep -REn 'os.system|subprocess.*shell=True|exec\(|child_process.exec' <server-src>
```

Shell built from args = critical injection.
items.how_to_fix: Use argv-array execution without a shell, validate/allowlist args, and never interpolate args into a shell string. Re-test injection.

items.title: Insufficient argument validation against schema
items.description: Even with a JSON schema, missing server-side validation (types, allowlists, length) lets malformed or hostile values reach dangerous sinks.
items.how_to_find: Test whether args outside the declared schema are accepted/processed.
items.how_to_fix: Strictly validate every argument against its schema server-side (type/range/allowlist), reject unexpected values, and don't trust model input. Re-test.

items.title: Dynamic code evaluation from arguments
items.description: Tools that eval/exec interpreted code (Python eval, JS Function, template engines) from arguments give direct code execution.
items.how_to_find:
Find eval/exec of tool arguments:

```
grep -REn '\beval\(|\bexec\(|new Function\(|pickle.loads' <server-src>
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove dynamic evaluation of arguments; use safe parsers/dispatch tables/allowlists. Re-test.

items.title: Path / filename arguments enabling traversal
items.description: Tools that take file paths can be steered to read/write outside intended directories when paths are not canonicalised and bounded.
items.how_to_find:
Test path/filename args for traversal:

```
# call a file tool with ../../etc/passwd
```
items.how_to_fix: Canonicalize + confine paths to an allowed base, reject `..`/absolute paths, and validate. Re-test traversal is blocked.

sections.title: Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery

items.title: URL fetched from tool argument without validation
items.description: A "fetch", "scrape", or "webhook" tool that requests an arbitrary URL from arguments enables SSRF into internal networks.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether a URL argument makes the server fetch arbitrary destinations:

```
# supply http://169.254.169.254/ or an internal URL as the arg
```
items.how_to_fix: Allowlist destinations, block internal/metadata ranges after DNS resolution, and validate URLs. Re-test SSRF is blocked.

items.title: Access to cloud metadata endpoints
items.description: SSRF reaching 169.254.169.254 (or equivalent) can harvest cloud instance credentials, a frequent privilege-escalation pivot.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether the server can reach cloud metadata:

```
# via a URL tool: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/
```
items.how_to_fix: Block metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254) from the server, enforce IMDSv2, and restrict egress. Re-test metadata is unreachable.

items.title: Dangerous URL schemes accepted
items.description: Accepting non-HTTP schemes (file://, gopher://, ftp://, dict://) broadens SSRF into file reads and protocol smuggling.
items.how_to_find: Test non-HTTP schemes (`file://`, `gopher://`, `ftp://`) in URL args.
items.how_to_fix: Allowlist only `http(s)`, reject dangerous schemes, and validate. Re-test scheme handling.

items.title: Blind SSRF / out-of-band egress
items.description: Even without a response body, a fetch tool can be used for blind SSRF and as a covert data-exfiltration channel via attacker-controlled URLs.
items.how_to_find:
Test for blind SSRF via out-of-band interaction:

```
# supply a Burp Collaborator / interactsh URL and watch for callbacks
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict egress by default (deny internal + arbitrary external), allowlist required destinations, and monitor. Re-test OOB callbacks don't fire.

sections.title: Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance

items.title: Untrusted / unverified server source
items.description: Installing a community MCP server from an unverified registry or repo runs arbitrary code with the user's privileges and exposes them to poisoned tools.
items.how_to_find: Check where each server comes from and whether its source/publisher is verified.
items.how_to_fix: Install servers only from trusted, verified sources (signed/known publishers), vet code before use, and avoid random registries. Re-verify sources.

items.title: Unpinned dependencies / install scripts
items.description: Servers installed via npx/uvx with unpinned versions or postinstall scripts can pull in malicious code at install or update time.
items.how_to_find:
Check for unpinned deps + install scripts that run on install:

```
grep -REn 'postinstall|preinstall|@latest' package.json <config>
```
items.how_to_fix: Pin dependencies + server versions, review install scripts, and install in a sandbox. Re-verify pinning.

items.title: Typosquatting / impersonation of servers
items.description: Attackers publish servers with names close to popular ones; a user connecting the wrong one hands over capability and data.
items.how_to_find: Check server package names for typosquats/impersonation of popular servers.
items.how_to_fix: Verify exact package names/publishers, use an allowlisted registry, and review new servers. Re-audit names.

items.title: No integrity verification on updates
items.description: Without signature/hash verification, a server update can swap benign code for malicious without the user noticing — a supply-chain rug pull.
items.how_to_find: Check whether server updates are integrity-verified (hash/signature) before applying.
items.how_to_fix: Verify signatures/hashes on updates, pin versions, and re-approve on change. Re-verify update integrity.

sections.title: Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools

items.title: Sensitive data egress through send/post tools
items.description: Tools that email, message, upload, or webhook data can exfiltrate secrets and PII the agent has access to, particularly under injection.
items.how_to_find: Test whether tools that send/post data can exfiltrate sensitive content to attacker destinations.
items.how_to_fix: Allowlist egress destinations, require confirmation for external sends, apply DLP, and least-privilege data access. Re-test exfil is blocked.

items.title: Encoded / covert exfiltration channels
items.description: Data can be smuggled in URL params, image links, DNS lookups, or markdown the client auto-fetches — covert channels that bypass obvious egress review.
items.how_to_find: Test covert channels (encoded data in URLs/params, DNS, timing).
items.how_to_fix: Monitor + restrict egress, detect encoded/anomalous outbound data, and allowlist destinations. Re-test covert channels.

items.title: Over-broad data access feeding egress
items.description: A tool that can read large or sensitive datasets, combined with any egress path, creates a high-volume exfiltration risk.
items.how_to_find: Check whether tools have broader data access than needed, enabling egress of sensitive data.
items.how_to_fix: Least-privilege data access per tool, separate read scope from egress tools, and minimize accessible data. Re-audit.

items.title: No egress monitoring / DLP
items.description: Without monitoring or data-loss-prevention on outbound tool actions, exfiltration goes undetected.
items.how_to_find: Check whether outbound data from the server is monitored (DLP).
items.how_to_fix: Add egress monitoring + DLP on server outbound traffic, alert on sensitive-data egress, and restrict destinations. Re-verify monitoring.

items.title: Secrets / tokens written to logs
items.description: Logging full requests or auth headers persists access tokens and API keys to disk where they can be harvested.
items.how_to_find:
Search server logs for secrets/tokens:

```
grep -REn 'token|secret|password|api[_-]?key' <logs>
```
items.how_to_fix: Redact secrets/tokens from logs, avoid logging auth material, and rotate any leaked. Re-check logs.

items.title: PII / payloads logged verbatim
items.description: Logging full tool arguments and results can persist personal data and confidential content, creating privacy and compliance exposure.
items.how_to_find: Check whether full request/response payloads (with PII) are logged verbatim.
items.how_to_fix: Log minimally, redact/mask PII, and set retention. Re-verify logs.

items.title: Log injection / forging via tool input
items.description: Unescaped tool inputs written to logs allow newline/control-character injection to forge or corrupt log entries, hampering forensics.
items.how_to_find: Test whether tool input with newlines/control chars can forge/inject log entries.
items.how_to_fix: Sanitize/encode log fields (strip CR/LF/control chars), use structured logging, and validate. Re-test log injection.

items.title: No security audit trail of tool calls
items.description: Absent an audit log of who invoked which tool with what effect, incident response and abuse detection are impossible.
items.how_to_find: Check whether tool calls are audited (who/what/when, args, result) for security review.
items.how_to_fix: Maintain a security audit trail of tool calls (identity, args, outcome) to a central store, and alert on anomalies. Re-verify audit coverage.

items.title: Filesystem access not jailed
items.description: A server (or filesystem tool) with access beyond its intended directory can read or write anything the host user can, including SSH keys and credentials.
items.how_to_find:
Test how much of the filesystem the server can access:

```
# via a file tool, attempt to read outside the intended dir
```

Full FS access = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Jail filesystem access to a specific allowed directory (chroot/container/bind-mount), reject traversal, and least-privilege. Re-test access is confined.

items.title: Unrestricted network egress from server
items.description: A server with open outbound network access can be used for SSRF, exfiltration, and C2; egress should be constrained to what the server needs.
items.how_to_find: Check the server's outbound network access.
items.how_to_fix: Restrict egress by default (allowlist), block internal/metadata, and monitor. Re-verify egress restrictions.

items.title: No process / container isolation
items.description: Running servers as the host user with no containerisation means a server RCE compromises the whole user environment.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether the server runs isolated (container/VM/user) vs directly on the host:

```
# is it a bare process as your user, or containerized?
```
items.how_to_fix: Run servers in isolated containers/VMs as non-root with seccomp/least privilege, so a compromise is contained. Re-verify isolation.

items.title: Secrets exposed via environment to server
items.description: Passing broad environment variables (cloud keys, tokens) into the server process exposes them to any code or tool the server runs.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether the server process environment contains unrelated secrets:

```
# inspect the launched process env / config for broad secrets
```
items.how_to_fix: Pass only the secrets a server needs (scoped), avoid exporting broad env secrets, use a secrets manager, and rotate exposed values. Re-verify the env.
